title: Fall of Granada and the end of the Reconquista
summary: On 2 January 1492 the Nasrid sultan Muhammad XII, known to Spanish tradition as Boabdil, handed the keys of the Alhambra to the Catholic Monarchs Isabella of Castile and Ferdinand of Aragon. Almost eight centuries after an Umayyad army had crossed the Strait of Gibraltar in 711, the last independent Muslim polity in the Iberian Peninsula ceased to exist. The victory made possible the Alhambra Decree expelling the Jews of Spain in March, the sponsorship of Christopher Columbus a few months later, and the reorientation of Castile into what would soon be a global empire.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Emirate of Granada had been founded in 1238 by Muhammad I ibn al-Ahmar, who took Granada, Malaga, and Almeria from the ruins of the Almohad caliphate and acknowledged the Christian king Ferdinand III of Castile as overlord in return for peace. For two and a half centuries his Nasrid successors ruled the last remnant of al-Andalus from the fortified palace-city of the Alhambra, which they enlarged in the fourteenth century into one of the great architectural ensembles of the medieval Mediterranean. They paid tribute to Castile in gold, kept a small standing army of Berber North African auxiliaries, and remained a rich if politically fragile client kingdom.

By the fifteenth century the geopolitical situation was worsening. Portuguese and Aragonese fleets had cut off much of Granada's Mediterranean trade; internal quarrels among the Nasrid family were routine; and Castile itself was reunited in 1469 by the marriage of Isabella of Trastamara, heir to Castile, and Ferdinand of Trastamara, heir to Aragon. When Isabella won the war of Castilian succession against her niece Juana in 1479, the two Catholic Monarchs, as they were later titled by the pope, controlled almost all Iberia except Portugal, Navarre, and Granada. Granada was next.

The immediate trigger was a raid. In December 1481 a Nasrid force under Ali Abu al-Hasan, the reigning sultan, seized the Castilian frontier fort of Zahara, killed many of its inhabitants, and enslaved the rest. In response Ferdinand and Isabella declared open war. From 1482 they launched a sustained campaign of siege warfare, using bombards cast in Flanders, engineers recruited from Italy and Germany, and a new professional armed force paid by the Santa Hermandad, the crown's rural militia and tax authority. The strategy was not to invade the mountainous emirate all at once but to reduce it town by town, cutting off each frontier stronghold, isolating its water and grain, and starving or storming it into submission.

Division inside Granada helped the Christian advance enormously. In 1482 Muhammad XII, son of Abu al-Hasan by an earlier wife, rebelled against his father and seized the city. In April 1483 he was captured by Castilian forces at the disastrous battle of Lucena, released after negotiations that turned him into a Castilian pensioner, and returned to Granada to fight both his father and his uncle Muhammad XIII, known as al-Zaghal, the Valiant. Between 1483 and 1489 a Nasrid civil war raged inside a war of national survival. Castilian and Aragonese forces exploited the confusion methodically. Ronda fell in 1485; Loja in 1486; Malaga in a terrible siege in the summer of 1487 that ended with most of its population enslaved. Baza fell in 1489. In December of that year al-Zaghal surrendered his remaining territories and left for North Africa. Only the city of Granada, still in Boabdil's hands, remained.

image.caption: The Court of the Lions in the Alhambra of Granada, the Nasrid palace complex surrendered on 2 January 1492. Chromolithograph after Owen Jones, 1842.
image.credit: Owen Jones and Jules Goury, 1842, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
In April 1491 Ferdinand and Isabella brought their army of perhaps 50,000 to 80,000 men into the Vega of Granada, the fertile plain that spreads north and west of the city, and built a fortified siege camp seven miles from the walls. When a fire in the camp destroyed the royal tent in July, they replaced the whole encampment with a permanent town of stone, which they called Santa Fe, Holy Faith. The town survives today. Muhammad XII, in the palaces on the hill of the Alhambra above the city, watched food and fodder run out through the autumn. Envoys began to move between Santa Fe and the Nasrid court.

The negotiations were conducted for Ferdinand by his secretary Hernando de Zafra and for the sultan by his vizier Abu al-Qasim al-Malih. The Capitulations of Granada, signed on 25 November 1491 with an initial period of confidentiality, granted extraordinarily generous terms to the defeated. The Muslim inhabitants of Granada would retain their religion, mosques, judges, and property; they would not be forced to wear distinguishing dress; those who wished to leave for North Africa would be provided with free passage. In exchange Muhammad XII surrendered the city and himself became a vassal, receiving a modest lordship in the Alpujarras.

Early in the morning of 2 January 1492 the formal handover took place. A Castilian delegation, led by the Count of Tendilla, was admitted through a postern into the Alhambra to occupy the fortress before word of the surrender could provoke resistance in the lower town. Later that morning Muhammad XII rode out of the palace with a small retinue and met the Catholic Monarchs on the plain near the Genil river. He dismounted, offered the keys of the city, and, in Latin sources, uttered a courteous formula of submission. The royal standards of Castile and Aragon and the banner of the crusade were raised together on the highest tower of the Alhambra, the Torre de la Vela. Bells and Te Deums rang across the Christian world. In Rome Pope Innocent VIII ordered public thanksgiving; in London Henry VII wrote a congratulatory letter; the news reached Constantinople within weeks.

According to Spanish tradition Muhammad XII paused on a mountain pass south of the city on his way to his new lordship, looked back at the Alhambra, and wept. His mother Aisha al-Hurra, in a line rendered famous in later Romantic literature, is said to have told him not to weep like a woman for what he had failed to hold like a man. The pass is still called the Puerto del Suspiro del Moro, the Pass of the Moor's Sigh. Modern historians treat the exchange as legend, but Muhammad XII did soon after leave for Morocco, where he lived in obscurity and, according to a plausible tradition, died at Fez in 1533.

image.caption: The Surrender of Granada by Francisco Pradilla Ortiz, 1882, showing Muhammad XII handing the keys to Ferdinand and Isabella.
image.credit: Francisco Pradilla y Ortiz (1848 to 1921), via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The terms of the Capitulations began to erode almost at once. In March 1492 the Catholic Monarchs signed the Alhambra Decree, drafted by the inquisitor general Tomas de Torquemada, ordering all Jews in the crowns of Castile and Aragon to convert to Christianity or leave the kingdoms by the end of July. Perhaps 40,000 to 100,000 Sephardic Jews chose exile, most heading to Portugal, Morocco, Ottoman territories, and the Italian city states. The Ottoman sultan Bayezid II is said to have marvelled that Ferdinand was called wise for impoverishing his own kingdom and enriching another. In April Ferdinand and Isabella agreed to sponsor the Genoese sea-captain Christopher Columbus, whom they had been keeping at court for years; his three ships would sail from Palos on 3 August 1492 and make landfall in the Bahamas on 12 October, opening a new hemisphere to the Castilian crown.

Granada itself passed under a bishop and a captain-general, and, for a short while, under the moderate rule of the Count of Tendilla as its first governor and the archbishop Hernando de Talavera. Talavera respected the Capitulations, learned Arabic, and pursued conversion by preaching and example. His policy was undone by the arrival in 1499 of the ambitious Francisco Jimenez de Cisneros, archbishop of Toledo, who ordered mass baptisms and public book burnings, including of Qur'ans and Islamic legal manuscripts. The Muslim quarter of the Albaicin rose in revolt in December 1499. The rising was crushed and, in 1502, an edict forced the last Muslims of Castile to convert or leave. Similar edicts followed in the crown of Aragon in 1526. The forced converts, called Moriscos, lived under suspicion for another century until Philip III expelled some 300,000 of them in 1609 to 1614, an act of ethnic cleansing whose demographic scars are visible in Valencia and Aragon to the present.

The Reconquista's success sat at the heart of a new Spanish self-understanding. The crown consolidated the Inquisition, established in 1478, as an instrument for enforcing religious uniformity. It carried the mental habits of the frontier war into the Atlantic, where conquistadors like Hernan Cortes and Francisco Pizarro would apply them to the peoples of Mexico and Peru. The Papacy responded to Iberia's new global reach by dividing the non-Christian world between Spain and Portugal in the bulls Inter caetera of 1493 and the Treaty of Tordesillas of 1494.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The fall of Granada is one of the pivots of European and Mediterranean history. It ended the political history of al-Andalus, a Muslim polity that had endured on the Iberian Peninsula for 780 years and given western Europe its main direct contact with the Arabic scientific and philosophical tradition. It sealed the union of the crowns of Castile and Aragon under a monarchy that would rule most of the Mediterranean, much of central Europe, the Americas, and, later, the Philippines. Modern Spain traces its origin as a unified state in large part to 1492.

The cultural legacies are more ambiguous. The Alhambra itself was preserved by its Castilian conquerors as a royal palace and today survives as one of the best-preserved medieval Islamic architectural ensembles in the world; the emperor Charles V, grandson of Ferdinand and Isabella, added a Renaissance palace within its walls without demolishing the older structures. Andalusian music, food, agricultural techniques, and vocabulary continued to shape Iberian culture long after the last Muslim rulers were gone. The Spanish language absorbed thousands of Arabic-origin words, from arroz and aceituna to Guadalquivir and Alcalá.

The darker inheritance has been slower to reckon with. The Alhambra Decree stood on the statute books of Spain until 1968, when the papal Church formally repealed the concordat provisions supporting it; a symbolic invitation to Sephardic Jews to reclaim Spanish citizenship was passed by the Spanish government in 2015 on the anniversary of the expulsion. The forced conversion and later expulsion of the Moriscos are subjects of active historical and public debate in Spain today. The image of a beleaguered Boabdil weeping over the lost Alhambra passed into Romantic literature, notably in Washington Irving's Tales of the Alhambra of 1832 and into modern novels like Salman Rushdie's The Moor's Last Sigh. Twelve months of 1492, one of them a decisive act of conquest at the beginning and another a landfall at the far end of the Atlantic in the autumn, changed the direction of world history.

worldContext.text: Ming China under the Hongzhi Emperor was in a period of relative calm and administrative reform after the difficulties of his predecessor. Neo-Confucian scholarship flourished; Beijing had been the fixed imperial capital for seven decades. The Zheng He voyages had ended nearly sixty years earlier, and imperial policy discouraged private overseas trade, though private Fujianese merchants continued to move through the ports of Southeast Asia.

The Ottoman sultan Bayezid II ruled from Constantinople an empire now stretching from the Danube to the Euphrates; he offered ships to carry Sephardic refugees from Spain. The Mamluk sultanate of Egypt under Qaitbay was in fiscal difficulty. Safavid preaching in Ardabil, which would produce Shah Isma'il's revolution in 1501, was gaining ground in the Turkic tribes of Anatolia and Azerbaijan. In Morocco the Wattasid dynasty ruled from Fez.

The Delhi Sultanate under the Lodi dynasty of Afghans held northern India from Bihar to the Punjab. In Central Asia the Timurid principalities were fragmenting into rival courts at Herat, under Sultan Husayn Bayqara, and Samarkand; the young prince Babur, born in 1483, was growing up in the Fergana valley and would one day found the Mughal Empire in India. The Vijayanagara Empire in the south was in its long high period.

In West Africa the Songhai Empire under Sunni Ali, who died in 1492, and soon under Askia Muhammad, was replacing the older Mali as the dominant power of the western Sudan. Portuguese ships had reached the Cape of Good Hope in 1488 under Bartolomeu Dias, opening the sea route to India and reshaping the Atlantic African coast. Along the Swahili coast the older commercial system of Kilwa and Mombasa was flourishing on the eve of Portuguese intervention.

In the Valley of Mexico the Aztec Triple Alliance under Ahuitzotl ruled a tributary empire from the Gulf to the Pacific; the Templo Mayor of Tenochtitlan had recently been enlarged in a great dedication that reportedly involved the sacrifice of many thousands. In Peru the Inca ruler Huayna Capac ruled a state stretching from Ecuador to Chile. The peoples of the Caribbean would meet Columbus's ships in October, an encounter that would transform world history.

In Aotearoa Maori pa fortifications and tribal warfare were well established. On Rapa Nui moai construction continued although the island's forests were vanishing. Polynesian societies across the eastern Pacific pursued long-standing fishing and horticultural economies. In highland New Guinea and across Melanesia intensive taro and sweet potato agriculture supported dense populations, and Aboriginal Australians maintained the fire-managed landscapes of the continent.

figures.name: Isabella I of Castile
figures.role: Queen of Castile and Leon from 1474, co-ruler with Ferdinand of a united Spain; sponsor of the final Granada campaign, the Alhambra Decree, and Columbus
figures.credit: Anonymous, c. 1490, Museo del Prado, Public Domain

figures.name: Ferdinand II of Aragon
figures.role: King of Aragon from 1479 and husband of Isabella; military commander of much of the Granada war
figures.credit: Michel Sittow, Kunsthistorisches Museum Vienna, Public Domain

figures.name: Muhammad XII (Boabdil)
figures.role: Last Nasrid sultan of Granada; surrendered the city on 2 January 1492 and later died in exile in Morocco

figures.name: Hernando de Talavera
figures.role: First archbishop of Granada; pursued a moderate policy of Arabic-speaking preaching and observance of the Capitulations, sidelined after 1499

figures.name: Francisco Jimenez de Cisneros
figures.role: Archbishop of Toledo whose harsh conversion campaign from 1499 provoked the Albaicin revolt and led to the forced conversion of Castilian Muslims in 1502

images.caption: The Surrender of Granada, oil on canvas by Francisco Pradilla Ortiz, 1882, showing Muhammad XII handing the keys to Ferdinand and Isabella. Palace of the Senate, Madrid.
images.credit: Francisco Pradilla y Ortiz (1848 to 1921), via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The Court of the Lions in the Alhambra of Granada, the palace complex handed over to the Catholic Monarchs on 2 January 1492. Chromolithograph after Owen Jones, 1842.
images.credit: Owen Jones and Jules Goury, 1842, via Wikimedia Commons
